posts.hook: There's a vision therapy tool used by military pilots and pro athletes that you can do at home with a $2 piece of string. It's called the Brock String.
posts.caption:
The Brock String trains binocular vision — the ability of both eyes to aim at the same point simultaneously.

Invented by Frederick Brock in the 1960s.

Still used today by:
→ Military aviators (USAF vision standards)
→ Professional athletes (MLB, NBA)
→ Vision therapists worldwide

Diagnoses AND treats:
- Convergence insufficiency
- Suppression (one eye 'switching off')
- Poor depth perception

The equipment: a string and 3 colored beads.

Guided exercise: axeo.vision/exercise

#BrockString #BinocularVision #VisionTherapy #Axeo #HealthTok #EyeHealth #SportsVision

posts.hook: The Brock String: how a piece of string exposes hidden binocular vision problems — and fixes them.
posts.caption:
Your eyes need to point at exactly the same spot to create a single, clear image.

This sounds simple. For 10–15% of people, it isn't.

Convergence insufficiency, suppression, and diplopia are all binocular vision disorders — and most go undiagnosed.

The Brock String reveals them in 30 seconds.

How it works:
1. Hold one end of a string to your nose
2. Extend it to a fixed point — 3 beads at different distances
3. Focus on the near bead: you should see the string form an X at the bead, with two string lines extending back
4. If you see only one line — one eye is suppressing
5. If the X is in front of or behind the bead — your convergence is off

It's also a training tool: focusing through each bead in sequence exercises vergence control.

Guided version at axeo.vision/exercise

#BrockString #ConvergenceInsufficiency #BinocularVision #VisionTherapy #Axeo #AxeoVision #EyeHealth

posts.hook: The Brock String test — take it right now and find out if your binocular vision is working correctly.
posts.caption:
10–15% of people have convergence insufficiency.

Most don't know.

The Brock String test reveals it in under a minute — and the equipment is a string with 3 beads.

What you'll see if your binocular vision is healthy:
→ String forms an X at whichever bead you focus on
→ Both 'arms' of the X extend forward and backward
→ X shifts as you change which bead you focus on

Signs of a problem:
→ Only one string line (suppression)
→ X falls in front of or behind the bead (convergence error)
→ Double images of beads you're not focusing on (normal — this is correct)

Axeo's exercise page walks you through the full Brock String protocol:
- Equipment list (string, 3 colored beads or markers)
- Setup guide
- What to look for
- Training sequence (5 cycles per bead, 3 beads)

Free at axeo.vision/exercise

posts.hook: 1 in 10 people's eyes don't work together correctly. Most have no idea. Here's the 30-second test to find out if you're one of them.
posts.caption:
Convergence insufficiency affects approximately 10% of the population.

It's the most common binocular vision disorder.

Yet it's rarely screened for in standard eye exams.

Symptoms that get misattributed:
→ Difficulty concentrating while reading
→ Words moving on the page
→ Headaches behind the eyes
→ One eye occasionally drifts
→ Double vision when fatigued

In children: frequently misdiagnosed as ADHD or learning disability.

The test takes 30 seconds:
→ Hold one finger up at arm's length
→ Slowly bring it toward your nose
→ Both eyes should track it perfectly until about 5–7cm
→ If one eye drifts or you lose single vision before that — test positive

For a proper protocol, the Brock String exercise at axeo.vision/exercise covers full testing and training for binocular vision.

Free, browser-based.

posts.hook: The Brock String is one of vision therapy's most powerful tools. It's been used since the 1960s. Almost no one outside clinical settings knows about it. Here's what it is and why it works:
posts.caption:
1/ Frederick Brock developed the Brock String in the 1960s as a diagnostic and therapeutic tool for binocular vision disorders. It remains a standard tool in vision therapy practices and sports vision programs today.

2/ What it tests: vergence — the coordinated inward (convergence) or outward (divergence) movement of both eyes to maintain single vision on a moving or near target. This is separate from accommodation (focus) and acuity (sharpness).

3/ The physics: hold a string from your nose to a fixed point with beads at near, mid, and far positions. When you focus on a bead, you should see TWO string lines (one from each eye) forming an X at the focused bead. If one line disappears, one eye is suppressing input. If the X is in the wrong location, vergence is off.

4/ What it reveals: convergence insufficiency (most common — X shifts forward), divergence excess, amblyopia suppression, and binocular instability. These are conditions that affect approximately 10–15% of the population but are rarely caught in standard exams.

5/ As a training tool: slowly walking focus from bead to bead exercises vergence control and vergence range. Research shows 12+ weeks of Brock String training significantly improves convergence near point and reduces symptoms in convergence insufficiency patients.

6/ Axeo has a guided Brock String protocol at axeo.vision/exercise — equipment list, setup, what to look for, and training sequence. Free, browser-based. No download.

If you read until here, your binocular vision is probably fine. But test it anyway.
posts.visual_notes: Scientific thread. Clear mechanism explanation. Diagnostic and therapeutic use cases. Credibility through historical context and clinical evidence.

posts.hook: The vision disorder misdiagnosed as a learning disability — and the tool that identifies it in 60 seconds.
posts.caption:
Convergence insufficiency is the most common binocular vision disorder.

It affects approximately 10% of people.

And it is routinely misdiagnosed — especially in children — as ADHD, dyslexia, or a general learning difficulty.

The reason: convergence insufficiency doesn't affect distance vision or acuity. It only affects near-point binocular function. Most standard eye exams don't test for it.

Symptoms in affected individuals:
→ Difficulty reading for extended periods
→ Words appearing to move or blur on a page
→ Headaches during or after reading
→ Avoidance of near work
→ Poor reading comprehension despite normal IQ

For working professionals, this presents differently:
→ Disproportionate screen fatigue
→ Difficulty switching between near and far visual targets
→ End-of-day diplopia (double vision) on text

The diagnostic tool most clinicians use: the Brock String. A string, 3 colored beads, and 60 seconds.

The same tool is used therapeutically — 12 weeks of structured training shows significant measurable improvement in clinical populations.

Axeo includes a guided Brock String protocol in the exercise library at axeo.vision/exercise — free, browser-based, with setup instructions and training sequence.

If this describes someone on your team, it may be worth flagging before assuming motivation or attention issues.
